_meta.purpose: Literature-derived clinical prior: which of the 45 model features SHOULD drive 30-day readmission, used as the answer key for the plausibility axis (NB11 spec sec 4).
_meta.anti_circularity_guarantee: Tiers and signs were assigned from peer-reviewed literature and established clinical reasoning ONLY, before inspecting any SHAP/LIME output of THIS model. Never seed this file from the model's own attributions.
_meta.validity_caveat: Author is not a clinician; this is a literature-grounded prior, not expert ground truth. Route past Minh-Khoi Pham (MKP) for clinical sanity check. Entries citing 'clinical_prior' have no peer-reviewed corpus source and rely on established clinical knowledge — flag these for expert review.

tiers.high: named as a top/primary predictor in peer-reviewed readmission literature
tiers.moderate: clinically relevant / supported as a contributing factor or comorbidity-burden component
tiers.not_expected: administrative or weak-proxy field; heavy model reliance here suggests a spurious shortcut

_meta.expected_sign: Direction the feature should push readmission risk. positive = higher value -> higher risk; negative = higher value -> lower risk; null = categorical or non-monotonic (judged on presence, not direction).
_meta.reference_top_k: The 'high'-tier features form the clinical reference top-K (K=10 core) used by plausibility agreement scoring.
_meta.arxiv_exclusion_note: Pham et al. CPE/Infection-Prevention paper (arXiv:2509.14942) and 'Prof MC Unprint Paper' deliberately NOT cited: rubric forbids arXiv/non-peer-reviewed sources, despite topical relevance.

baig2019_acuteml.ref: Baig MM, Hua N, Zhang E, Robinson R, et al. Machine Learning-based Risk of Hospital Readmissions: Predicting Acute Readmissions within 30 Days of Discharge. IEEE EMBC, 2019.
baig2019_acuteml.supports: LOS, Charlson Comorbidity Index, ED visits (6mo), gender, age, number of diagnoses, prior acute admissions (90/180-day), acute/emergency admission type

li2024_medicare_lstm.ref: Li X, Liu S, Yu D, et al. Predicting 30-Day Hospital Readmission in Medicare Patients: Insights from an LSTM Deep Learning Model. IEEE CBASE, 2024.
li2024_medicare_lstm.supports: LOS (OR 1.015), Charlson comorbidity score (OR 1.127), prior readmissions within 6 months (OR 1.199), gender (female OR 0.802)

pham2024_irish.ref: Pham M-K, Mai TT, Crane M, Ebiele M, Brennan R, Ward ME, Geary U, McDonald N, Bezbradica M. Forecasting Patient Early Readmission from Irish Hospital Discharge Records Using Conventional Machine Learning Models. Diagnostics (MDPI), 2024.
pham2024_irish.supports: diagnosis codes as key predictors, cancer/malignant neoplasm, COPD (J44), socioeconomic/social factors

tang2023_mmstgnn.ref: Tang S, Tariq A, Dunnmon JA, Sharma U, Elugunti P, Rubin DL, Patel BN, Banerjee I. Predicting 30-Day All-Cause Hospital Readmission Using Multimodal Spatiotemporal Graph Neural Networks. IEEE J Biomedical and Health Informatics, 27(4), 2023.
tang2023_mmstgnn.supports: laboratory values (sodium, creatinine, troponin, anion gap), cancer + vascular + heart disease comorbidity, discharge disposition

alnazari2025_renal.ref: Alnazari N, Alanazi OI, Alosaimi MO, et al. Development of explainable artificial intelligence based machine learning model for predicting 30-day hospital readmission after renal transplantation. BMC Nephrology, 26:203, 2025.
alnazari2025_renal.supports: Length of stay as the single most predominant predictor (38% SHAP contribution). NOTE: transplant-specific cohort; cite for LOS only.

clinical_prior.ref: Established clinical knowledge / standard readmission risk reasoning; NOT from the peer-reviewed corpus.
clinical_prior.supports: polypharmacy and high-risk medication classes, electrolyte/glucose derangement, recency-of-discharge dynamics. FLAG for MKP/expert confirmation.

features.rationale: Length of stay is repeatedly the strongest or among the strongest readmission predictors.
Same construct as los_days at finer granularity; longer stay signals greater acuity and readmission risk.
Very recent prior admissions strongly elevate readmission risk.
Prior acute admissions in a 90-day window are an explicit listed predictor.
Longer-window prior utilisation is an established readmission driver.
Cumulative prior admissions (frequent utilisation) is a top predictor.
Number of diagnoses / diagnostic burden is a listed key predictor.
Acute/emergency admission type is an explicit risk predictor vs elective.
Congestive heart failure is a classic high-readmission comorbidity; heart disease flagged as key comorbidity.
Cancer / malignant neoplasm emerges as a key contributor to increased readmission risk.
COPD (J44) identified as a key chronic-condition contributor to readmission.
Chronic kidney disease is a high-burden comorbidity associated with readmission.
Diabetes contributes to comorbidity burden captured by the Charlson index.
Myocardial infarction is a Charlson component; comorbidity burden drives risk.
Peripheral vascular disease contributes to overall Charlson comorbidity burden.
Cerebrovascular disease is a Charlson component within comorbidity burden.
Dementia is a Charlson component; raises care complexity and readmission risk.
Abnormal laboratory results signal physiological instability at discharge.
Volume of lab testing proxies workup intensity / acuity.
Elevated creatinine (renal impairment) is a named top lab feature.
Sodium derangement named among top lab features; abnormal in either direction matters.
Sodium derangement named among top lab features; abnormal in either direction matters.
Low hemoglobin (anemia) reflects illness severity; lower value -> higher risk.
Elevated WBC signals active infection/inflammation at discharge.
Potassium derangement is clinically significant in either direction.
Potassium derangement is clinically significant in either direction.
Glucose dysregulation (hypo/hyper) reflects metabolic instability.
Glucose dysregulation (hypo/hyper) reflects metabolic instability.
Age is a listed predictor; older patients generally carry higher readmission risk (effect can be non-linear).
Sex shows a measurable readmission odds difference (female OR 0.802).
Medication count proxies treatment complexity and comorbidity burden.
Polypharmacy is an established readmission risk factor (adverse events, adherence).
High-risk medication class (bleeding/monitoring burden).
Insulin use proxies more severe diabetes / management complexity.
Opioid use marks pain/illness severity and adverse-event risk.
Active/recent infection treatment marks ongoing infection risk.
Shorter time since last discharge -> higher readmission risk (recency).
Recent prior admission flags elevated short-term readmission risk.
Frequent-utiliser flag derived from prior-admission burden, a top predictor.
Discharge disposition (e.g. home vs facility) informs post-discharge risk.
Admission source relates to acuity/route of presentation.
Insurance/payer is a recognised socioeconomic determinant of readmission.

Race appears as a modelled factor in readmission literature, but is fairness-sensitive: it likely proxies social determinants rather than biology. Interpret with the with/without-race fairness analysis, not as a clinical cause.
At best a weak proxy for social support; not a direct clinical predictor. Heavy reliance here suggests a spurious shortcut.
Administrative field / weak access-barrier proxy; no direct clinical causal role. Heavy reliance here suggests a spurious shortcut.
